You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Player (white+light green, 4x4) moves 4 cells per action. 3 charcoal-framed targets with blue centers. 1 blue platform with gray interior. Targets change color when player is adjacent, revert when player leaves. Pink bar at bottom is HUD. Goal likely involves collecting/activating all 3 targets, possibly with SPACE.
- Plan: Move to left target and try SPACE to collect it.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The player moved LEFT. Now let me check the position and plan to collect the left target. The left target is at (28,16)-(31,19). I need to get adjacent to it and press SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check player position after LEFT
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['W', 'N']:
        print(f"Player {n['color']}: boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 12:
        print(f"Target: boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Left target is at (28,16)-(31,19)
# Need to get adjacent to it
# Player needs to be at rows 32-35 (below target) or rows 24-27 (above) or cols 12-15 (left) or cols 20-23 (right)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Player is at:
- Green: (36,24)-(39,26) 
- White: (36,27)-(39,31)
So the player spans rows 36-39, cols 24-31.
Left target is at (28,16)-(31,19). To get adjacent:
- I need to be at rows 32-35 (below target) with cols 16-19 (same horizontal range)
- Or at cols 20-23 (right of target) with rows 28-31 (same vertical range)
Currently player is at rows 36-39, cols 24-31. 
- UP would move to rows 32-35, cols 24-31. Then I'd be below the target area but at cols 24-31, which is to the right of the target (target cols 16-19).
- I need to also go LEFT to align with the target's columns.
Let me think about this more carefully:
- Player is 4 rows tall (36-39) and spans cols 24-31 (8 cols wide since we collected one target)
- Wait, the player white is 16 pixels with boundary [[36, 27], [36, 31], [39, 31], [39, 27]] - that's rows 36-39, cols 27-31, which is 4x5 = 20 cells... but it says 16 pixels. Let me reconsider.
- Actually the boundary is [[36, 27], [36, 31], [39, 31], [39, 27]] which means rows 36-39 and cols 27-31. That's a 4x4 = 16 pixel area. Makes sense.
- The green part is [[36, 24], [36, 26], [39, 26], [39, 24]] which is rows 36-39, cols 24-26. That's 4x3 = 12 pixels.
- Total player width: cols 24-31 (8 columns), height: rows 36-39 (4 rows)
To get adjacent to the left target at (28,16)-(31,19):
- I need to go UP to rows 32-35 (below target) and LEFT to cols 16-19
- Or I need to go UP to rows 28-31 and LEFT to cols 12-15 (left of target)
Let me try: UP to get to rows 32-35, then LEFT to get to cols 16-19, then UP to get to rows 28-31 (target rows), then... hmm, this is getting complicated.
Actually, let me think about it differently. The target is at (28,16)-(31,19). To be adjacent:
- Below: rows 32-35, cols 16-19
- Above: rows 24-27, cols 16-19
- Left: rows 28-31, cols 12-15
- Right: rows 28-31, cols 20-23
Player is at rows 36-39, cols 24-31. 
- UP → rows 32-35, cols 24-31 (still to the right of target)
- LEFT → rows 32-35, cols 20-27 (getting closer)
- LEFT → rows 32-35, cols 16-23 (now adjacent to target from below/right)
Actually wait, the player is 8 cols wide (24-31). Moving LEFT by 4 would put it at cols 20-27. Moving LEFT again would put it at cols 16-23. At that point, the player would span cols 16-23, and the target is at cols 16-19. So the player would overlap with the target horizontally. But the player is at rows 32-35 and the target is at rows 28-31. So the player would be directly below the target, adjacent to it.
Let me try: UP, LEFT, LEFT, then SPACE. But first let me check if the platform blocks movement.
Actually, the platform is at (28,28)-(31,39). If I go UP first, the player would be at rows 32-35, cols 24-31. The platform is at rows 28-31, so the player wouldn't overlap with it. Then LEFT to cols 20-27, then LEFT to cols 16-23. The player would be at rows 32-35, cols 16-23, which is below the target at (28,16)-(31,19). 
Wait, but the player is 8 cols wide. At cols 16-23, the player would span cols 16-23. The target is at cols 16-19. So the player's left side (cols 16-19) would be directly below the target. That should work for adjacency.
Let me try: UP, LEFT, LEFT, SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 36-39, cols 24-31
# Left target at rows 28-31, cols 16-19
# Plan: UP to rows 32-35, then LEFT x2 to cols 16-23, then SPACE
# This puts player below the target, adjacent to it

action(['UP', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
